title: Air Conditioners
description: On a strip of land of length $$$n$$$ there are $$$k$$$ air conditioners: the $$$i$$$-th air conditioner is placed in cell $$$a_i$$$ ($$$1 \le a_i \le n$$$). Two or more air conditioners cannot be placed in the same cell (i.e. all $$$a_i$$$ are distinct). Each air conditioner is characterized by one parameter: temperature. The $$$i$$$-th air conditioner is set to the temperature $$$t_i$$$. Example of strip of length $$$n=6$$$, where $$$k=2$$$, $$$a=[2,5]$$$ and $$$t=[14,16]$$$. For each cell $$$i$$$ ($$$1 \le i \le n$$$) find it's temperature, that can be calculated by the formula $$$$$$\min_{1 \le j \le k}(t_j + |a_j - i|),$$$$$$ where $$$|a_j - i|$$$ denotes absolute value of the difference $$$a_j - i$$$. In other words, the temperature in cell $$$i$$$ is equal to the minimum among the temperatures of air conditioners, increased by the distance from it to the cell $$$i$$$. Let's look at an example. Consider that $$$n=6, k=2$$$, the first air conditioner is placed in cell $$$a_1=2$$$ and is set to the temperature $$$t_1=14$$$ and the second air conditioner is placed in cell $$$a_2=5$$$ and is set to the temperature $$$t_2=16$$$. In that case temperatures in cells are: temperature in cell $$$1$$$ is: $$$\min(14 + |2 - 1|, 16 + |5 - 1|)=\min(14 + 1, 16 + 4)=\min(15, 20)=15$$$; temperature in cell $$$2$$$ is: $$$\min(14 + |2 - 2|, 16 + |5 - 2|)=\min(14 + 0, 16 + 3)=\min(14, 19)=14$$$; temperature in cell $$$3$$$ is: $$$\min(14 + |2 - 3|, 16 + |5 - 3|)=\min(14 + 1, 16 + 2)=\min(15, 18)=15$$$; temperature in cell $$$4$$$ is: $$$\min(14 + |2 - 4|, 16 + |5 - 4|)=\min(14 + 2, 16 + 1)=\min(16, 17)=16$$$; temperature in cell $$$5$$$ is: $$$\min(14 + |2 - 5|, 16 + |5 - 5|)=\min(14 + 3, 16 + 0)=\min(17, 16)=16$$$; temperature in cell $$$6$$$ is: $$$\min(14 + |2 - 6|, 16 + |5 - 6|)=\min(14 + 4, 16 + 1)=\min(18, 17)=17$$$. For each cell from $$$1$$$ to $$$n$$$ find the temperature in it.
input_spec: The first line contains one integer $$$q$$$ ($$$1 \le q \le 10^4$$$) — the number of test cases in the input. Then test cases follow. Before each test case, there is an empty line. Each test case contains three lines. The first line contains two integers $$$n$$$ ($$$1 \le n \le 3 \cdot 10^5$$$) and $$$k$$$ ($$$1 \le k \le n$$$) — the length of the strip of land and the number of air conditioners respectively. The second line contains $$$k$$$ integers $$$a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_k$$$ ($$$1 \le a_i \le n$$$) — positions of air conditioners on the strip of land. The third line contains $$$k$$$ integers $$$t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_k$$$ ($$$1 \le t_i \le 10^9$$$) — temperatures of air conditioners. It is guaranteed that the sum of $$$n$$$ over all test cases does not exceed $$$3 \cdot 10^5$$$.
output_spec: For each test case output $$$n$$$ integers separated by space: temperatures of air in cells.

routes.name: Left-right DP sweep
routes.description: Split the absolute value into two cases (AC to the left, AC to the right) and compute each with a single linear sweep, then take the element-wise minimum.

observations.title: Brute force is too slow
observations.description: The naive O(nk) approach of checking every AC for every cell will TLE because n and k can each be up to 3*10^5. We need a way to compute all n answers in O(n) or O(n log n).

hints.nudge: What happens if you try to compute the formula directly for each cell?
hints.question: With n and k both up to 3*10^5, how many operations does the brute force take? Is there a way to avoid re-examining every AC for every cell?
hints.formal: Brute force is O(nk) which can be ~9*10^10. We need to exploit the structure of the distance function to compute answers in O(n).

observations.title: Place AC temperatures on the strip
observations.description: Create an array c of length n, set c[a_j - 1] = t_j for each AC and infinity elsewhere. The answer for cell i is then min over all j of (c[j] + |j - i|) where c[j] < infinity.

hints.nudge: Instead of thinking about k air conditioners, can you think about n cells?
hints.question: What if you created an array indexed by cell position, placing each AC's temperature at its position and infinity elsewhere? How does the formula simplify?
hints.formal: Let c[i] = t_j if AC j is at position i+1, else infinity. Then ans[i] = min over all j of (c[j] + |j - i|). This reformulation lets us think about sweeps over the array.

observations.title: Decompose absolute value into left and right
observations.description: The absolute value |j - i| equals (i - j) when j <= i and (j - i) when j >= i. So the answer is min(best from the left, best from the right) where each considers only one direction.

hints.nudge: Absolute value has two cases. What if you handled each case separately?
hints.question: If you split the minimum into contributions from ACs to the left of cell i and ACs to the right, what does each sub-expression look like? Does it suggest a sweep direction?
hints.formal: ans[i] = min(L[i], R[i]) where L[i] = min over j<=i of (c[j] + i - j) and R[i] = min over j>=i of (c[j] + j - i). L sweeps left-to-right, R sweeps right-to-left.

observations.title: Left sweep recurrence
observations.description: When sweeping left to right, L[i] = min(L[i-1] + 1, c[i]). Either the best contribution from the left grows by 1 as we move one cell further, or cell i itself has an AC.

hints.nudge: If you know the best temperature reachable from the left at cell i-1, what is it at cell i?
hints.question: Consider L[i] = min over j<=i of (c[j] + i - j). How does L[i] relate to L[i-1]? What happens to the distance term when i increases by 1?
hints.formal: L[i] = min(L[i-1] + 1, c[i]). Proof: min over j<=i of (c[j] + i - j) = min(min over j<=i-1 of (c[j] + (i-1-j) + 1), c[i]) = min(L[i-1] + 1, c[i]).

observations.title: Symmetric right sweep
observations.description: R[i] = min(R[i+1] + 1, c[i]) by the same logic, sweeping from right to left. The final answer for each cell is min(L[i], R[i]).

hints.nudge: You handled ACs to the left. How do you handle ACs to the right?
hints.question: Can you write an analogous recurrence for R[i] that sweeps right-to-left? How do you combine L and R for the final answer?
hints.formal: R[i] = min(R[i+1] + 1, c[i]), computed for i = n-1 down to 0. Final answer: ans[i] = min(L[i], R[i]). Total time: O(n) per test case.

routes.name: Multi-source BFS / shortest paths
routes.description: Model cells as nodes in a path graph with edge weight 1, treat each AC as a source with initial distance t_j, and run a multi-source shortest path computation.

observations.title: Reframe as shortest path from virtual sources
observations.description: Create a virtual super-source S connected to each AC position a_j with edge weight t_j. Then ans[i] = shortest path from S to cell i, where adjacent cells are connected with weight 1.

hints.nudge: The formula t_j + |a_j - i| looks like a distance. Can you think of this as a graph problem?
hints.question: If cells 1..n are nodes on a line with weight-1 edges, and each AC is a source with initial cost t_j, what graph algorithm computes min(t_j + dist(a_j, i)) for all i?
hints.formal: Add a super-source S with edge S -> a_j of weight t_j for each AC. Then ans[i] = dist(S, i) in the resulting weighted graph. Since the graph is a path plus star edges, this simplifies.

observations.title: Multi-source relaxation on a line
observations.description: On a path graph, shortest paths from multiple sources can be computed by initializing distances at the sources and relaxing edges in both directions, which is equivalent to the two-sweep DP.

hints.nudge: Dijkstra on a path graph with uniform edges is just a sweep. What does multi-source Dijkstra become?
hints.question: If you initialize dist[a_j] = t_j for each AC and infinity elsewhere, and then relax the edge (i, i+1) of weight 1 in both directions, what recurrence do you get?
hints.formal: Initialize c[a_j-1] = t_j, else infinity. Forward pass: L[i] = min(L[i-1]+1, c[i]). Backward pass: R[i] = min(R[i+1]+1, c[i]). Answer: min(L[i], R[i]). This is Bellman-Ford on a path = two sweeps.

observations.title: Priority queue approach is unnecessary
observations.description: Although you could use a priority queue (Dijkstra) with all ACs as initial nodes, the path graph structure means simple linear sweeps achieve the same result in O(n) without any data structure overhead.

hints.nudge: Do you actually need a heap for this graph shape?
hints.question: What special property does a path graph have that makes Dijkstra's priority queue unnecessary? How many times can each edge be relaxed?
hints.formal: On a path graph, each node has at most 2 neighbors. A left-to-right sweep relaxes all rightward edges; a right-to-left sweep relaxes all leftward edges. Two passes suffice, giving O(n) without a heap.
